back_translation: So the king and Haman went to join the banquet with Queen Esther.
While they drank wine on that second day, the king again asked, 'Queen Esther, what is your petition? It will be granted. And your request? Even up to half the kingdom, it will be done.'
Queen Esther replied, 'O king, if I have found favor in your sight, and if it pleases you, please grant me my life as my petition, and my people as my request,

for I and my people have been sold to destruction, slaughter, and annihilation. If we had merely been sold as male and female slaves, I would have kept silent, because such distress would not be worth troubling the king.'
King Ahasuerus asked Queen Esther, 'Who is he, and where is he, who would dare to do such a thing?'
Queen Esther said, 'The adversary and enemy is this wicked Haman!' And Haman was terrified before the king and the queen.
The king rose in his fury and went from the wine banquet out to the palace garden, while Haman stayed to beg Queen Esther for his life, for he saw that the king had determined to punish him.

When the king returned from the palace garden to the wine-banquet hall, he saw Haman flung down on the couch where Esther was reclining. The king said, 'Would he even assault the queen before me in this house?' As soon as the words left the king's mouth, the servants covered Haman's face.

Harbona, one of the eunuchs attending the king, said, 'Behold, at Haman's house there is a gallows fifty cubits high that Haman built for Mordecai, who gave the report that saved the king's life.' The king said, 'Hang Haman on it.'
So they hanged Haman on the gallows he had prepared for Mordecai. Then the king's fury subsided.
